1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	
5	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
6	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
7	
8	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
9	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
10	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
11	\[
12	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
13	  \qquad
14	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
15	\]
16	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
17	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
18	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
19	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
20	
21	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
22	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
23	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
24	% groups)
25	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
26	% SOURCE QUOTE:
27	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
28	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
29	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
30	% abelian groups defined by the formula
31	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
32	% \end{dfn}
33	%  [\dots]
34	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
35	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
36	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
37	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
38	% topologies  by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
40	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
41	%  \end{dfn}"
42	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
43	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
44	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
45	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
46	homomorphisms.}
47	
48	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
49	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
50	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
51	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
52	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
53	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
54	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
55	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
56	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
57	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
58	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
59	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
60	structure. This is the content of
61	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
62	
63	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
64	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
65	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
66	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
67	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
68	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
69	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
70	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
71	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
72	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
73	\begin{enumerate}
74	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
75	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
76	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
77	    isomorphism classes;
78	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
79	    for \(\otimes\);
80	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
81	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
82	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
83	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
84	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
85	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
86	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
87	\end{enumerate}
88	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
89	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
90	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
91	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
92	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
93	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
94	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
95	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
96	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
97	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
98	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
99	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
100	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
101	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
102	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
103	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
104	supplied.
105	
106	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
107	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
108	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
109	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
110	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
111	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
112	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
113	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
114	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
115	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
116	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
118	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
119	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
121	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
122	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
123	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
124	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
125	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
126	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
127	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
128	avoids. The
129	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
130	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
131	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
132	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
133	
134	\section{Mathlib API survey}
135	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
136	
137	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
138	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
139	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
140	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
141	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
142	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
143	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
144	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
145	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
146	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
147	source.
148	
149	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
150	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
151	monoidal-category instance
152	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
153	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
154	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
155	\[
156	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
157	\]
158	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
159	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
160	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
161	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
162	% SOURCE QUOTE:
163	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
164	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
165	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
166	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
167	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
168	%   ...
169	%   tensorUnit := unit _
170	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
171	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
172	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
173	\textit{Source: [Mathlib], \texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct} /
174	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}, in
175	\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean}, L104--L125.}
176	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
177	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
178	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
179	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
180	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
181	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
182	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
183	
184	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
185	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
186	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
187	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
188	the whiskering-stability data:
189	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
190	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
191	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
192	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
193	% SOURCE QUOTE:
194	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
195	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
196	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
197	%  ...
198	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
199	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
200	%  ...
201	% noncomputable instance :
202	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."

206	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
207	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
208	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
209	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
210	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
211	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
212	hypothesis chain.

214	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
215	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
216	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
217	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
218	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
219	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
220	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
221	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
222	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
223	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
224	% SOURCE QUOTE:
225	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
226	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
227	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
228	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
229	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
230	%     intro Φ
231	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
232	%     infer_instance)"
233	\textit{Source: [Mathlib],
234	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
235	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
236	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
237	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
238	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
239	\(A\).
240	
241	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
242	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
243	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
244	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
245	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
246	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
247	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
248	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
249	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
250	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
251	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
252	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
253	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
254	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
255	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
256	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
257	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
258	critical path.
259	
260	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
261	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
262	is already present in Mathlib: the file
263	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
264	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
265	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
266	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
267	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
268	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
269	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
270	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
271	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
272	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
273	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
274	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
275	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
276	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
277	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
278	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
279	
280	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
281	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
282	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
283	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
284	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
285	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
286	independently of any upstream timing.
287	
288	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
289	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
290	
291	\begin{definition}
292	
293	\leanok
294	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
295	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
297	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
298	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
299	  \[
300	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
301	  \]
302	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
303	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
304	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
305	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
306	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
307	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
308	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
309	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
310	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
311	  canonical compatibility
312	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
313	     \;\cong\;
314	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
315	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
316	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
317	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
318	
319	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
320	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
321	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
322	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
323	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
324	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
325	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
326	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	
331	\leanok
332	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
333	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
335	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
336	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
337	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
338	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
339	  \[
340	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
342	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
343	  \]
344	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
345	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
346	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
347	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
348	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
349	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
350	  \[
351	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
352	    \qquad
353	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
354	  \]
355	  the associator
356	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
357	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
358	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
359	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
360	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
361	\end{lemma}
362	
363	\begin{proof}
364	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
365	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
366	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
367	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
368	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
369	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
370	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
371	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
372	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
373	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
374	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
375	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
376	\end{proof}
377	
378	\begin{remark}
379	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
380	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
381	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
382	        lem:whisker_of_W}
383	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
384	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
385	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
386	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
387	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
388	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
389	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
390	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
391	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
392	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
393	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
394	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
395	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
396	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
397	
398	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
399	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
400	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
401	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
402	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
403	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
404	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
405	  fixed-base presheaves in
406	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
407	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
408	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
409	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
410	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
411	  it.
412	
413	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
414	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
415	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
416	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
417	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
418	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
419	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
420	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
421	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
422	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
423	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
424	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
425	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
426	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
427	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
428	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
429	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
430	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
431	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
432	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
433	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
434	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
435	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
436	\end{remark}
437	
438	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
439	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
440	
441	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
442	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
443	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
444	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
445	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
446	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
447	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
448	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
451	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
452	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
453	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
454	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
455	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
456	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
457	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
458	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
459	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
460	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
461	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
462	to the off-path route~(e).
463	
464	\begin{lemma}
465	
466	\leanok
467	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
468	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
469	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
470	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
471	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
472	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
473	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
474	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
475	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
476	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
477	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
478	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
479	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
480	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
481	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
482	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
483	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
484	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
485	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
486	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
487	  \[
488	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
489	      \;\;:=\;\;
490	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
491	  \]
492	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
493	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
494	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
495	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
496	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
497	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
498	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
499	\end{lemma}
500	
501	\begin{proof}
502	
503	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
504	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
505	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
506	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
507	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
508	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
509	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
510	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
511	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
512	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
513	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
514	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
515	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
516	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
517	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
518	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
519	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
520	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
521	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
522	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
523	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
524	
525	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
526	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
527	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
528	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
529	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
530	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
531	\end{proof}

533	\begin{lemma}
534	
535	\leanok
536	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
537	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
538	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
539	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
540	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
541	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
542	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
543	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
544	  morphism
545	  \[
546	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
547	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
548	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
549	  \]
550	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
551	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
552	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
553	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
554	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
555	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
556	\end{lemma}
557	
558	\begin{proof}
559	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
560	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
561	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
562	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
563	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
564	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
565	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
566	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
567	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
568	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
569	  isomorphism}
570	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
571	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
572	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
573	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
574	  therefore extension of scalars along
575	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
576	
577	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
578	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
579	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
580	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
581	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
582	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
583	  the abstract pullback functor.
584	
585	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
586	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
587	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
588	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
589	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
590	   \;\cong\;
591	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
592	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
593	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
594	  comparison
595	  \[
596	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
597	      \;\longrightarrow\;
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
599	      \;\otimes\;
600	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
601	  \]
602	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
603	
604	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
605	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
606	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
607	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
608	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
609	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
610	  % "\begin{lemma}
611	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
612	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
613	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
614	  % \begin{enumerate}
615	  % \item The functor
616	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
617	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
618	  % \item The functor
619	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
620	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
621	  %  [\dots]
622	  % \end{enumerate}
623	  % \end{lemma}
624	  %  [\dots]
625	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
626	  % of functors, see
627	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
628	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
629	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
630	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
631	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
632	
633	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
634	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
635	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
636	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
637	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
638	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
639	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
640	
641	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
642	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
643	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
644	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
645	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
646	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
647	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
648	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
649	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
650	  \otimes_S N)\).
651	
652	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
653	  \[
654	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
655	  \]
656	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
657	  presheaf-level adjunction
658	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
659	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
660	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
661	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
662	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
663	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
664	  composing the resulting comparison with
665	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
666	
667	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
668	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
669	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
670	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
671	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
672	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
673	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
674	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
675	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
676	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
677	
678	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
679	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
680	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
681	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
682	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
683	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
684	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
685	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
686	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
687	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
688	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
689	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
690	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
691	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
692	\end{proof}
693	
694	\begin{lemma}
695	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
696	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
697	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
698	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
699	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
700	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
701	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
702	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
703	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
704	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
705	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
706	
707	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
708	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
709	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
710	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
711	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
712	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
713	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
714	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
715	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
716	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
717	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
718	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
719	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
720	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
721	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
722	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
723	
724	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
725	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
726	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
727	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
728	  restriction-of-scalars functor
729	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
730	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
731	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
732	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
733	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
734	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
735	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
736	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
737	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
738	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
739	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
740	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
741	
742	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
743	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
744	\end{lemma}
745	
746	\begin{lemma}
747	
748	\leanok
749	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
750	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
751	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
752	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
753	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
754	  product: the canonical map
755	  \[
756	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
757	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
758	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
759	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
760	  \]
761	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
762	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
763	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
764	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
765	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
766	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
767	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
768	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
769	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
770	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
771	\end{lemma}
772	
773	\begin{lemma}
774	
775	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
776	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
777	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
778	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
779	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
780	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
781	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
782	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
783	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
784	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
785	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
786	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
787	  Mathlib). Then:
788	  \begin{enumerate}
789	    \item the lax tensorator
790	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
791	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
792	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
793	    \item the lax unit
794	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
795	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
796	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
797	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
798	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
799	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
800	  \end{enumerate}
801	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
802	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
803	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
804	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
805	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
806	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
807	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
808	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
809	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
810	\end{lemma}
811	
812	\begin{proof}
813	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
814	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
815	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
816	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
817	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
818	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
819	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
820	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
821	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
822	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
823	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
824	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
825	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
826	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
827	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
828	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
829	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
830	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
831	
832	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
833	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
834	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
835	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
836	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
837	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
838	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
839	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise

857	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank

888	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
889	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
890	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
891	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
892	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
893	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
894	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
895	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
896	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
897	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
898	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
899	  left-whiskered morphism
900	  \[
901	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
902	  \]
903	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
904	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
905	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
906	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
907	
908	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
909	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
910	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
911	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
912	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
913	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
914	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
915	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
916	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
917	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
918	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
919	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
920	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
921	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
922	\end{lemma}
923	
924	\begin{proof}
925	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
926	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
927	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
928	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
929	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
930	
931	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
932	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
933	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
934	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
935	  \[
936	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
937	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
938	  \]
939	
940	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
941	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
942	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
943	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
944	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
945	
946	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
947	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
948	  linear maps (Mathlib
949	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
950	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
951	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
952	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
953	
954	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
955	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
956	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
957	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
958	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
959	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
960	\end{proof}

1073	along `g : X ⟶ Y` carries `termRingMap X f` to `termRingMap Y f`; equivalently the
1074	images of a global scalar `f ∈ R(T)` are compatible with restriction. This is the
1075	sole ingredient making `globalSMul f` a genuine morphism of presheaves of modules. -/
1076	lemma termRingMap_naturality {X Y : Cᵒᵖ} (g : X ⟶ Y) (f) :
1077	    (ConcreteCategory.hom ((R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).map g))
1078	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT X)) f)
1079	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT Y)) f := by
1080	  have h : (hT.from X.unop).op ≫ g = (hT.from Y.unop).op := by
1081	    apply Quiver.Hom.unop_inj; apply hT.hom_ext
1082	  change (ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map g)) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map (hT.from X.unop).op)) f) = _
1083	  rw [show termRingMap hT Y = R.map (hT.from Y.unop).op from rfl, ← h, Functor.map_comp]; rfl
1084	
1085	/-- **Multiplication by a global scalar `f ∈ R(T)` as an endomorphism of `N`.** At an
1086	object `Y`, it is scalar multiplication by `termRingMap Y f ∈ R(Y)` on `N(Y)`; the
1087	naturality square commutes by `termRingMap_naturality` and the semilinearity of the
1088	restriction maps of `N` (`PresheafOfModules.map_smul`). This is the central object: it
1089	gives the `R(T)`-action on `Hom(M, N)` by post-composition (`homModule`). -/
1090	noncomputable def globalSMul (N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
1091	    (f : (R.obj (Opposite.op T) : Type u)) : N ⟶ N :=
1092	  PresheafOfModules.Hom.mk
1093	    (fun Y => ModuleCat.ofHom
1094	      (LinearMap.lsmul (R.obj Y : Type u) (N.obj Y) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT Y)) f)))
1095	    (by
1096	      intro X Y g; ext m
1097	      change (LinearMap.lsmul (R.obj Y : Type u) (N.obj Y)
1098	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT Y)) f)) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (N.map g)) m)
1099	          = (ConcreteCategory.hom (N.map g)) ((LinearMap.lsmul (R.obj X : Type u) (N.obj X)
1100	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT X)) f)) m)
1101	      rw [LinearMap.lsmul_apply, LinearMap.lsmul_apply]; erw [PresheafOfModules.map_smul]
1102	      rw [termRingMap_naturality]; rfl)
1103	
1104	variable {N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
1105	
1106	/-- The section-wise action of `globalSMul f`: at `Y`, it is scalar multiplication by
1107	`termRingMap Y f`. -/
1108	lemma globalSMul_hom_apply (f : (R.obj (Opposite.op T) : Type u)) (Y : Cᵒᵖ) (m : N.obj Y) :
1109	    ((globalSMul hT N f).app Y).hom m = ((ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT Y)) f) • m := rfl
1110	
1111	/-- `globalSMul 1 = 𝟙`. -/
1112	lemma globalSMul_one : globalSMul hT N 1 = 𝟙 N := by
1113	  ext Y m; rw [globalSMul_hom_apply, map_one, one_smul]; rfl
1114	
1115	/-- `globalSMul 0 = 0`. -/
1116	lemma globalSMul_zero : globalSMul hT N 0 = 0 := by
1117	  ext Y m; rw [globalSMul_hom_apply, map_zero, zero_smul]; rfl
1118	
1119	/-- `globalSMul` is additive in the scalar. -/
1120	lemma globalSMul_add (f g : (R.obj (Opposite.op T) : Type u)) :
1121	    globalSMul hT N (f + g) = globalSMul hT N f + globalSMul hT N g := by
1122	  ext Y m; rw [add_app, globalSMul_hom_apply, _root_.map_add, add_smul]; rfl
1123	
1124	/-- `globalSMul` turns ring multiplication into composition (the scalar endomorphisms
1125	commute, so the order is immaterial). -/
1126	lemma globalSMul_mul (f g : (R.obj (Opposite.op T) : Type u)) :
1127	    globalSMul hT N (f * g) = globalSMul hT N g ≫ globalSMul hT N f := by
1128	  ext Y m; rw [comp_app, globalSMul_hom_apply, map_mul, mul_smul, ModuleCat.hom_comp,
1129	    LinearMap.comp_apply, globalSMul_hom_apply, globalSMul_hom_apply]
1130	
1131	/-- **The `R(T)`-module structure on `Hom(M, N)` of presheaves of modules over a base
1132	category `C` with a terminal object `T`.** The action `f • φ := φ ≫ globalSMul f` scales
1133	a morphism by post-composing with multiplication by the global scalar `f ∈ R(T)`; the
1134	module axioms reduce to the `globalSMul_{one,zero,add,mul}` ring-homomorphism facts and
1135	the bilinearity of composition (`Preadditive`). This is the module carried by each value
1136	of the slice internal hom `ℋom(M, N)(U)` of blueprint `def:presheaf_internal_hom`
1137	(take `C =` the restricted site over `U`, with terminal `U`, so `R(T) = R(U)`).
1138	Project-local: Mathlib has the fixed-ring internal hom but no `PresheafOfModules`-level
1139	internal hom for the varying structure sheaf. -/
1140	@[implicit_reducible]
1141	noncomputable def homModule (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
1142	    Module (R.obj (Opposite.op T) : Type u) (M ⟶ N) where
1143	  smul f φ := φ ≫ globalSMul hT N f
1144	  one_smul φ := by change φ ≫ globalSMul hT N 1 = φ; rw [globalSMul_one, Category.comp_id]
1145	  mul_smul f g φ := by
1146	    change φ ≫ globalSMul hT N (f * g) = (φ ≫ globalSMul hT N g) ≫ globalSMul hT N f
1147	    rw [globalSMul_mul, Category.assoc]
1148	  smul_zero f := by change (0 : M ⟶ N) ≫ globalSMul hT N f = 0; rw [Limits.zero_comp]
1149	  zero_smul φ := by change φ ≫ globalSMul hT N 0 = 0; rw [globalSMul_zero, Limits.comp_zero]
1150	  smul_add f φ ψ := by
1151	    change (φ + ψ) ≫ globalSMul hT N f = φ ≫ globalSMul hT N f + ψ ≫ globalSMul hT N f
1152	    rw [Preadditive.add_comp]
1153	  add_smul f g φ := by
1154	    change φ ≫ globalSMul hT N (f + g) = φ ≫ globalSMul hT N f + φ ≫ globalSMul hT N g
1155	    rw [globalSMul_add, Preadditive.comp_add]
1156	
1157	/-! ### The slice value at an object `U` via the over-category `Over U`
1158	
1159	The slice internal hom `ℋom(M, N)(U) := ModuleCat.of (R(U)) (M|_U ⟶ N|_U)` of
1160	blueprint `def:presheaf_internal_hom` is realized object-by-object here. The
1161	restriction `M|_U` is `PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀` along the over-category
1162	projection `Over.forget U : Over U ⥤ C`; the over-category has terminal object
1163	`Over.mk (𝟙 U)` (`Over.mkIdTerminal`), at which the restricted ring is `R(U)`
1164	(by `rfl`), so the `R(U)`-module structure on `M|_U ⟶ N|_U` is exactly `homModule`
1165	applied to that terminal. -/
1166	
1167	/-- **Restriction of a presheaf of modules to the over-category `Over U`.** This is
1168	the `M|_U` of the slice internal-hom formula: `PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀` along
1169	the over-category projection `Over.forget U`. The new base presheaf of rings is
1170	`(Over.forget U).op ⋙ R`, whose value at the terminal `Over.mk (𝟙 U)` is `R(U)`. -/
1171	noncomputable def restr (U : C)
1172	    (M : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
1173	    PresheafOfModules.{u} (((Over.forget U).op ⋙ R) ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1174	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ (Over.forget U) (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)).obj M
1175	
1176	/-- **The slice internal-hom value at `U`: `Hom(M|_U, N|_U)` as an `R(U)`-module.**
1177	This is the `R(U)`-module underlying `ℋom(M, N)(U)` of blueprint
1178	`def:presheaf_internal_hom`: the morphism group of the over-category restrictions
1179	`M|_U ⟶ N|_U`, equipped with the `homModule` action for the terminal object
1180	`Over.mk (𝟙 U)` of `Over U`. The full presheaf `internalHom` (the assembly of these
1181	values over the restriction maps `V ⟶ U`) was assembled iter-220 (`internalHom`). -/
1182	@[implicit_reducible]
1183	noncomputable def internalHomObjModule (U : C)
1184	    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
1185	    Module (R.obj (Opposite.op U) : Type u) (restr U M ⟶ restr U N) :=
1186	  homModule (R := (Over.forget U).op ⋙ R) (T := Over.mk (𝟙 U)) Over.mkIdTerminal
1187	    (restr U M) (restr U N)
1188	
1189	/-- **The restriction map of the presheaf internal hom.** For a morphism `g : V ⟶ U`
1190	of `C`, restricting a morphism `φ : M|_U ⟶ N|_U` of restricted presheaves of modules
1191	along `Over.map g : Over V ⥤ Over U` yields a morphism `M|_V ⟶ N|_V`. Realised as
1192	`(pushforward₀ (Over.map g) …).map φ`; the target base ring presheaf
1193	`(Over.map g).op ⋙ (Over.forget U).op ⋙ R` is definitionally `(Over.forget V).op ⋙ R`,
1194	so the result has the expected type `restr V M ⟶ restr V N`. This is the
1195	further-restriction map `φ ↦ φ|_V` of blueprint `lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`. -/
1196	noncomputable def restrictionMap {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
1197	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
1198	    (φ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) : restr V M ⟶ restr V N :=
1199	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ (Over.map g)
1200	    ((Over.forget U).op ⋙ (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map φ
1201	
1202	/-- **`restrictionMap` is additive.** Part of the additivity assertion of blueprint
1203	`lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`. -/
1204	lemma restrictionMap_add {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
1205	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
1206	    (φ ψ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) :
1207	    restrictionMap g (φ + ψ) = restrictionMap g φ + restrictionMap g ψ := by
1208	  ext1 X; rfl
1209	
1210	/-- **`restrictionMap` preserves zero.** -/
1211	lemma restrictionMap_zero {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
1212	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)} :
1213	    restrictionMap g (0 : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) = 0 := by
1214	  ext1 X; rfl
1215	
1216	/-- Helper: the component of a morphism of presheaves of modules at two *equal*
1217	objects agrees up to `HEq`. Proven by `subst`. Used to discharge the
1218	pseudofunctoriality coherence of `restrictionMap` (`Over.map` is only functorial in
1219	its argument up to `Over.mapId_eq` / `Over.mapComp_eq`). -/
1220	private lemma hom_app_heq {B : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}} {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} B}
1221	    (φ : M ⟶ N) {X Y : Cᵒᵖ} (h : X = Y) : HEq (φ.app X) (φ.app Y) := by
1222	  subst h; rfl
1223	
1224	/-- **Functoriality of `restrictionMap`: identity.** -/
1225	lemma restrictionMap_id {U : C}
1226	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
1227	    (φ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) :
1228	    restrictionMap (𝟙 U) φ = φ := by
1229	  ext1 X
1230	  exact eq_of_heq (hom_app_heq φ (by rw [Over.mapId_eq]; rfl))
1231	
1232	/-- **Functoriality of `restrictionMap`: composition.** For `g : V ⟶ U`, `h : W ⟶ V`,
1233	restricting along `h ≫ g` is restricting along `g` then along `h`. -/
1234	lemma restrictionMap_comp {U V W : C} (g : V ⟶ U) (h : W ⟶ V)
1235	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
1236	    (φ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) :
1237	    restrictionMap (h ≫ g) φ = restrictionMap h (restrictionMap g φ) := by
1238	  ext1 X
1239	  exact eq_of_heq (hom_app_heq φ (by rw [Over.mapComp_eq]; rfl))
1240	
1241	/-- **`restrictionMap` respects composition of morphisms** (functoriality of
1242	`pushforward₀.map`). -/
1243	lemma restrictionMap_comp_hom {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
1244	    {M N P : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
1245	    (φ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) (ψ : restr U N ⟶ restr U P) :
1246	    restrictionMap g (φ ≫ ψ) = restrictionMap g φ ≫ restrictionMap g ψ :=
1247	  Functor.map_comp _ _ _
1248	
1249	/-- **Restriction commutes with the global-scalar endomorphism.** For `g : V ⟶ U`
1250	and a global scalar `r ∈ R(U)`, restricting the multiplication-by-`r` endomorphism of
1251	`N|_U` yields the multiplication-by-`R(g)(r)` endomorphism of `N|_V`. This is the heart
1252	of the semilinearity of `restrictionMap` (blueprint `lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`):
1253	both sides act on each section by scalar multiplication, and the scalars agree by
1254	functoriality of `R` (`termRingMap_naturality`). -/
1255	lemma restrictionMap_globalSMul {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
1256	    {N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
1257	    (r : (R.obj (Opposite.op U) : Type u)) :
1258	    restrictionMap g (globalSMul (R := (Over.forget U).op ⋙ R) (T := Over.mk (𝟙 U))
1259	        Over.mkIdTerminal (restr U N) r)
1260	      = globalSMul (R := (Over.forget V).op ⋙ R) (T := Over.mk (𝟙 V))
1261	        Over.mkIdTerminal (restr V N) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map g.op)) r) := by
1262	  ext1 Y
1263	  ext m
1264	  rw [globalSMul_hom_apply]
1265	  erw [globalSMul_hom_apply]
1266	  congr 1
1267	  simp only [termRingMap, Functor.comp_map]
1268	  have hmor : (Over.forget U).op.map
1269	        (Over.mkIdTerminal.from ((Over.map g).op.obj Y).unop).op
1270	      = g.op ≫ (Over.forget V).op.map (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop).op := by
1271	    apply Quiver.Hom.unop_inj
1272	    change Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from ((Over.map g).obj Y.unop))
1273	      = Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop) ≫ g
1274	    have e1 : Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from ((Over.map g).obj Y.unop))
1275	        = ((Over.map g).obj Y.unop).hom := by simp
1276	    have e2 : Over.Hom.left (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop) = Y.unop.hom := by simp
1277	    rw [e1, e2]
1278	    simp [Over.map_obj_hom]
1279	  rw [hmor]
1280	  erw [← CommRingCat.comp_apply, ← R.map_comp]
1281	  rfl
1282	
1283	/-- **`restrictionMap` packaged as an additive group homomorphism** on the morphism
1284	groups, using `restrictionMap_zero` and `restrictionMap_add`. This is the value of the
1285	underlying `Ab`-presheaf morphism of `internalHom`. -/
1286	noncomputable def restrictionMapAddHom {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
1287	    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
1288	    (restr U M ⟶ restr U N) →+ (restr V M ⟶ restr V N) where
1289	  toFun := restrictionMap g
1290	  map_zero' := restrictionMap_zero g
1291	  map_add' := restrictionMap_add g
1292	
1293	/-- **The underlying `Ab`-valued presheaf of `internalHom M N`**: `U ↦ (M|_U ⟶ N|_U)`
1294	with the further-restriction maps. Functoriality is `restrictionMap_id` /
1295	`restrictionMap_comp`. This is piece (a)+(b)+(c) of blueprint `def:presheaf_internal_hom`
1296	at the abelian-group level; the `R(U)`-module refinement is added by `internalHom`. -/
1297	noncomputable def internalHomPresheaf
1298	    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
1299	    Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{max u uC vC} where
1300	  obj X := AddCommGrpCat.of (restr X.unop M ⟶ restr X.unop N)
1301	  map {X Y} f := AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (restrictionMapAddHom f.unop M N)
1302	  map_id X := by
1303	    apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
1304	    refine AddMonoidHom.ext fun φ => ?_
1305	    exact restrictionMap_id φ
1306	  map_comp {X Y Z} f f' := by
1307	    apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
1308	    refine AddMonoidHom.ext fun φ => ?_
1309	    exact restrictionMap_comp f.unop f'.unop φ
1310	
1311	/-- **Semilinearity of `restrictionMap`** in the form consumed by
1312	`PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf`: for `f : X ⟶ Y` in `Cᵒᵖ`, a global scalar
1313	`r ∈ R(X)` and `m : M|_{X} ⟶ N|_{X}`, `restrictionMap f.unop (r • m) =
1314	R(f)(r) • restrictionMap f.unop m`, where the `•`'s are the slice-value module
1315	actions (`internalHomObjModule`). This is `lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`'s
1316	compatibility datum; it follows from `restrictionMap_comp_hom` and
1317	`restrictionMap_globalSMul`. -/
1318	lemma restrictionMap_smul {X Y : Cᵒᵖ} (f : X ⟶ Y)
1319	    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
1320	    (r : (R.obj X : Type u)) (m : restr X.unop M ⟶ restr X.unop N) :
1321	    restrictionMap f.unop
1322	        (letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop M N; r • m)
1323	      = (letI := internalHomObjModule Y.unop M N;
1324	          (ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map f)) r • restrictionMap f.unop m) := by
1325	  change restrictionMap f.unop (m ≫ globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal (restr X.unop N) r)
1326	    = restrictionMap f.unop m
1327	        ≫ globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal (restr Y.unop N) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map f)) r)
1328	  rw [restrictionMap_comp_hom, restrictionMap_globalSMul, Quiver.Hom.op_unop]
1329	
1330	end InternalHom
1331	
1332	/-! ### Assembly of the presheaf internal hom over a single-universe base
1333	
1334	`PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf` ties the underlying `Ab`-presheaf to the ground ring
1335	presheaf `R`'s universe (`Type u`), but for a general base category the morphism groups
1336	`M|_U ⟶ N|_U` live in `Type (max u uC vC)`. The two coincide exactly when the base
1337	category is single-universe (`Category.{u, u}`), which is the case for the topological
1338	site `Opens X` underlying the structure sheaf of a scheme. The full presheaf internal
1339	hom is therefore assembled in this specialised universe context. -/
1340	
1341	namespace InternalHom
1342	
1343	section Assembly
1344	
1345	variable {D : Type u} [Category.{u, u} D] {R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
1346	
1347	/-- **The presheaf internal hom `ℋom(M, N)`** (blueprint `def:presheaf_internal_hom`):
1348	the presheaf of `R`-modules assembled from the slice value modules
1349	`internalHomObjModule` (a), the further-restriction maps `internalHomPresheaf` (b)(c),
1350	and the semilinearity datum `restrictionMap_smul`, via `PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf`.
1351	Stated over a single-universe base (e.g. the topological site `Opens X`); see the
1352	section note for the universe constraint. -/
1353	noncomputable def internalHom
1354	    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
1355	    PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1356	  @PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf D _ (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)
1357	    (internalHomPresheaf M N)
1358	    (fun X => internalHomObjModule X.unop M N)
1359	    (fun {_ _} f r m => restrictionMap_smul f M N r m)
1360	
1361	end Assembly
1362	
1363	end InternalHom
1364	
1365	namespace InternalHom
1366	
1367	open CategoryTheory Limits Opposite
1368	
1369	universe vC uC
1370	
1371	variable {C : Type uC} [Category.{vC} C] {R : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
1372	  {T : C} (hT : IsTerminal T)
1373	
1374	/-- **`termRingMap` at the terminal object itself is the identity.** The unique
1375	`T → T` is the identity, so the induced ring map `R(T) → R(T)` is `id`. This is the
1376	fact that the global-scalar action evaluated at the terminal section is the scalar
1377	itself, used to prove the evaluation map of `internalHomEval` is `R(U)`-bilinear. -/
1378	lemma termRingMap_terminal (f : (R.obj (op T) : Type u)) :
1379	    (ConcreteCategory.hom (termRingMap hT (op T))) f = f := by
1380	  have hid : hT.from T = 𝟙 T := hT.hom_ext _ _
1381	  simp only [termRingMap, unop_op, hid, op_id, R.map_id]
1382	  rfl
1383	
1384	end InternalHom
1385	
1386	/-! ### The presheaf dual `M^∨ := ℋom(M, R)`
1387	
1388	The presheaf dual is the internal hom into the monoidal unit (the structure presheaf
1389	viewed as a module over itself), assembled in the same single-universe context as
1390	`InternalHom.internalHom`. Blueprint `def:presheaf_dual`. -/
1391	
1392	section Dual
1393	
1394	open InternalHom Opposite
1395	
1396	variable {D : Type u} [Category.{u, u} D] {R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
1397	
1398	/-- **The presheaf dual `M^∨ := ℋom(M, R)`** (blueprint `def:presheaf_dual`): the
1399	internal hom into the monoidal unit `𝟙_` (the structure presheaf `R` viewed as a module
1400	over itself / the regular representation). The value
1401	`M^∨(U) = ModuleCat.of (R(U)) (M|_U ⟶ R|_U)` is the `R(U)`-module of `R|_U`-linear
1402	functionals on `M|_U`, the presheaf-of-modules analogue of the fixed-ring linear dual
1403	`Module.Dual R M = (M ⟶ R)` that serves as the `⊗`-inverse of an invertible module.
1404	Project-local: Mathlib has the fixed-ring dual but no `PresheafOfModules`-level dual for
1405	the varying structure sheaf. -/
1406	noncomputable def dual
